I want to be clear at the outset.
Regulatory clarity and digital environment is legitimate.
Ensuring overseas providers are subject
to New Zealand law is a reasonable objective.
However, this bill materially expands state enforcement
power in ways that without strong safeguards creates civil
liberties and market confidence risks.
My submission focuses on three issues.
One, interception capability
and decryption, two license revocation
as an enforcement weapon.
Three, due process
and proportion proportionality,
one interception capability and encryption.
The bill expands the territorial scope
of the telecommunications act so that overseas providers
and network operators are clearly subject
to interception obligations,
even though the bill does not explicitly mandate weakening
encrypt encryption.
The practical reality is this,
when interception compliance is backed
by strong enforcement sanctions,
providers whose systems rely on strict end-to-end encryption
may face structural pressure to redesign their systems.
Strong encryption is not optional technology.
It protects ordinary new Zealand's new citizens from cyber
crime businesses, from commercial espionage lawyers
and journalists, from breaches of confidentiality,
families from identity theft.
If regulatory uncertainty leads
to weakened encryption architecture,
or if providers withdraw secure services from the
New Zealand market, new Zealanders are less
safe, not more safe.
This bill should therefore include an explicit clause
stating that compliance mechanisms must not require systemic
vulnerabilities or general weakening
of end-to-end encryption That would provide clarity without
obstructing lawful interception under proper authority.
Two, license revocation and restriction powers.
The new part seven. A enforcement regime allows the
secretary to revoke or permanently restrict radio
and spectrum licenses where there is serious non-compliance.
This is not a MI minor sanction
spectrum access is foundational
to telecommunications operations.
The bill also allows enforcement consequences to flow
through to licenses held
by third parties if provider relies on them.
That means regulatory action against one entity may affect
intermediaries and end users.
When interception compliance is linked to the potential loss
of spectrum rights, the leverage is significant
without a clearly articulated proportionality framework
on the face of the act.
The risk is that license revocation becomes a blunt
compliance tool rather than a last resort.
If revocation is available, it must be clearly structured
as one necessary.
Two. Proportionate three demonstrably the least
intrusive option available.
Those requirements should not be implied.
They should be explicit.
Three due process and evidential standards.
The bill permits the secretary
to rely solely on summary information
provided by surveillance agencies when considering
enforcement action,
where the sanction may include revocation
or permanent restriction of spectrum licenses.
That evidential threshold is low. This creates two risks.
First, a due process risk for providers.
Second, a confidence risk for the broader market.
Severe regulatory sanctions require strong procedural
safeguards for revocation
or permanent restriction decisions.
The bill should require a higher evidential standard than
summary material alone.
Clear recorded reasoning addressing proportionality
and structured consideration
of downstream impact on New Zealand customers.
That is not an obstruction. It is good regulatory design.
Four, offshore provider confidence.
The bill's purpose is
to ensure overseas providers are subject to New Zealand law.
However, New Zealand is a small market.
If compliance risk is uncertain, technically incompatible
with certain encryption models
or tied to severe discretionary enforcement powers,
providers may choose to limit services here.
The result would be, would not be improved sovereignty.
It would be reduced service availability
and potentially reduced cybersecurity resilience.
Clear statutory safeguards reduce
that risk while preserving enforcement capability.
Conclusion, this bill shifts power that may be justified,
but when power shifts,
safeguards must strengthen accordingly.
I recommended the committee amend the bill to include
an explicit non weakening of encryption safeguard.
A statutory proportionality
and least intrusive means test for license sanctions,
stronger evidential requirements for vocational
or permanent restriction, clear protection of third party
and end user interests.
Limited transparency reporting on use
of these new enforcement powers.
With those amendments, the bill can achieve its policy
objective without undermining civil liberties,
cybersecurity, or market confidence.

equal treatment cross providers is reasonable,
